Example: Acoustic heating"] HT --> S1 -->|Yes| E1 S1 -->|No| S2 S2 -->|First order in t| P1 S2 -->|Second order in t| H1 style HT fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px style S1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C style S2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style E1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px style H1 fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Classify the following PDE and state the type with justification:** $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + 2\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial y} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0$$ **Model Answer:** Compare with the standard form $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} = 0$: - $A = 1$ - $2B = 2 \Rightarrow B = 1$ - $C = 1$ Compute the discriminant: $$\Delta = B^2 - AC = (1)^2 - (1)(1) = 1 - 1 = 0$$ Since $\Delta = 0$, the PDE is **PARABOLIC**. *(3 marks: 1 for coefficient identification, 1 for discriminant calculation, 1 for correct conclusion)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Why is the classification of PDEs important in computational heat transfer?** **Model Answer:** The classification of PDEs is crucial in computational heat transfer for the following reasons: 1. **Boundary Condition Selection**: The type of PDE dictates what boundary/initial conditions are mathematically and physically admissible. Elliptic PDEs require closed boundaries, parabolic PDEs require an initial condition plus spatial BCs, and hyperbolic PDEs require two initial conditions. 2. **Numerical Method Selection**: Each type demands a different numerical approach — elliptic problems are solved by iterative methods (Gauss-Seidel, SOR), parabolic problems by time-marching schemes (explicit, Crank–Nicolson), and hyperbolic problems by characteristic-based methods (upwind, Lax–Wendroff). 3. **Stability Behavior**: The discriminant sign predicts the stability character of the chosen numerical scheme. For example, the explicit scheme for the heat equation has a stability condition linked directly to the parabolic nature. 4. **Physical Insight**: Classification immediately tells whether the phenomenon is steady (elliptic), diffusive (parabolic), or wave-like (hyperbolic). *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** *Starting from the general second-order linear PDE, derive the condition used to classify it as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Classify each of the following PDEs:* *(i)* $u_{xx} - 2u_{xy} + u_{yy} = 0$ *(ii)* $u_t = \alpha^2 u_{xx}$ (one-dimensional transient heat conduction) *(iii)* $u_{xx} + u_{yy} + u_{zz} = 0$ (3D Laplace equation) --- **Model Solution to (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:** The general second-order linear PDE in two independent variables is: $$A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} + D u_x + E u_y + F u = G(x,y) \quad \text{...(1)}$$ The principal (highest-order) part is: $$A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ Substitute a trial solution $u = e^{\lambda(\alpha x + \beta y)}$: $$u_x = \lambda \alpha u, \quad u_{xx} = \lambda^2 \alpha^2 u$$ $$u_y = \lambda \beta u, \quad u_{yy} = \lambda^2 \beta^2 u$$ $$u_{xy} = \lambda^2 \alpha \beta u$$ Substituting into equation (2) and dividing by $u$: $$A \alpha^2 + 2B \alpha \beta + C \beta^2 = 0 \quad \text{...(3)}$$ This is the **characteristic equation**. Dividing by $\beta^2$ and setting $\xi = \alpha/\beta$: $$A \xi^2 + 2B \xi + C = 0 \quad \text{...(4)}$$ Applying the quadratic formula: $$\xi = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - AC}}{A} \quad \text{...(5)}$$ The classification is based on the discriminant $\Delta = B^2 - AC$: - If $\Delta < 0$: Roots are complex conjugates → **Elliptic** - If $\Delta = 0$: One real double root → **Parabolic** - If $\Delta > 0$: Two distinct real roots → **Hyperbolic** **Final classification criterion:** $$\boxed{\Delta = B^2 - AC \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{cases} \Delta < 0 & \text{Elliptic} \\ \Delta = 0 & \text{Parabolic} \\ \Delta > 0 & \text{Hyperbolic} \end{cases}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for ansatz, 2 for characteristic equation, 2 for quadratic formula, 1 for final classification table)* --- **Model Solution to (b) — Classification [7 Marks]:** **(i)** $u_{xx} - 2u_{xy} + u_{yy} = 0$ Compare: $A = 1, \, 2B = -2 \Rightarrow B = -1, \, C = 1$ $$\Delta = B^2 - AC = (-1)^2 - (1)(1) = 1 - 1 = 0$$ Since $\Delta = 0$, the PDE is **PARABOLIC**. **(ii)** $u_t = \alpha^2 u_{xx}$ Rearrange: $\alpha^2 u_{xx} - u_t = 0$, or $\alpha^2 u_{xx} + 0 \cdot u_{xt} + 0 \cdot u_{tt} - u_t = 0$ The independent variables are $x$ and $t$. Compare with $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xt} + C u_{tt} = 0$: $$A = \alpha^2, \quad 2B = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0, \quad C = 0$$ $$\Delta = B^2 - AC = 0 - (\alpha^2)(0) = 0$$ Since $\Delta = 0$, the PDE is **PARABOLIC**. (This is the famous 1D heat equation.) **(iii)** $u_{xx} + u_{yy} + u_{zz} = 0$ This is a 3D PDE. The discriminant classification generalizes to multiple variables using the **eigenvalues** of the coefficient matrix. The coefficient matrix is: $$M = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ The eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 1$, **all of the same sign** (all positive). Hence the PDE is **ELLIPTIC**. *Equivalent 2D analysis:* For the 2D part $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$: $A=1, B=0, C=1$, $\Delta = -1 < 0$ → Elliptic. By extension, the 3D equation remains **ELLIPTIC**. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 for the three sub-parts)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain with mathematical justification why the general 2D heat equation* $u_t = \alpha(u_{xx} + u_{yy})$ *is parabolic. Hence state the canonical form and three boundary conditions required for a well-posed problem.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *For the PDE* $3 u_{xx} + 7 u_{xy} + 4 u_{yy} = 0$, *classify it and find the characteristic directions. Comment on the type of solution you would expect.* --- **Model Solution to (a) [7 Marks]:** The given equation is $u_t - \alpha u_{xx} - \alpha u_{yy} = 0$. Rearranging with independent variables $x$ and $t$ (treating $y$ as a parameter for the moment): $$-\alpha u_{xx} + 0 \cdot u_{xt} + 0 \cdot u_{tt} = u_t \quad \text{(treating first-order as lower order)}$$ Looking at the principal part with $x$ and $t$ as independent variables: $$A = -\alpha, \quad 2B = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0, \quad C = 0$$ $$\Delta = B^2 - AC = 0 - (-\alpha)(0) = 0$$ Since $\Delta = 0$, the PDE is **PARABOLIC**. **Canonical form (Heat Equation):** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)}$$ **Required conditions for a well-posed parabolic problem:** 1. **Initial condition (IC)**: $u(x, 0) = f(x)$ — temperature distribution at time $t = 0$. 2. **Boundary condition at $x = 0$**: $u(0, t) = g_1(t)$ or $-\left.k \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x=0} = g_1(t)$ (Dirichlet or Neumann). 3. **Boundary condition at $x = L$**: $u(L, t) = g_2(t)$ or $-\left.k \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x=L} = g_2(t)$. These three conditions (one IC + two spatial BCs) make the transient 1D heat conduction problem **well-posed** in the sense of Hadamard. *(7 marks: 2 for classification, 2 for canonical form, 3 for the well-posed conditions)* --- **Model Solution to (b) [7 Marks]:** Given PDE: $3 u_{xx} + 7 u_{xy} + 4 u_{yy} = 0$ Compare with $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} = 0$: $$A = 3, \quad 2B = 7 \Rightarrow B = 3.5, \quad C = 4$$ **Discriminant calculation:** $$\Delta = B^2 - AC = (3.5)^2 - (3)(4) = 12.25 - 12 = 0.25$$ $$\Delta = 0.25 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{HYPERBOLIC}$$ **Finding the characteristic directions:** From the characteristic equation $A \alpha^2 + 2B \alpha \beta + C \beta^2 = 0$: $$3 \alpha^2 + 7 \alpha \beta + 4 \beta^2 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula with $\xi = \alpha/\beta$: $$\xi = \frac{-7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 48}}{6} = \frac{-7 \pm 1}{6}$$ So the two real roots are: $$\xi_1 = \frac{-7 + 1}{6} = \frac{-6}{6} = -1$$ $$\xi_2 = \frac{-7 - 1}{6} = \frac{-8}{6} = \frac{-4}{3}$$ **Characteristic directions** in the $xy$-plane: $$y = -x + c_1 \quad \text{and} \quad y = -\frac{4}{3}x + c_2$$ **Expected Solution Behavior:** Since the PDE is **hyperbolic**, it has **two distinct real characteristic families**. Solutions will exhibit **wave-like propagation** along these characteristics. The general solution is of the form: $$u(x, y) = F(y + x) + G\left(y + \frac{4}{3}x\right)$$ where $F$ and $G$ are arbitrary functions determined by boundary/initial conditions. Disturbances propagate along the two characteristic lines, and information travels at finite speeds (unlike elliptic problems where disturbances are felt instantaneously). *(7 marks: 2 for classification, 3 for characteristic equation, 2 for solution form and physical interpretation)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:** > > 1. **The "2B Trap"** — Students often forget that the mixed derivative is written as $2B u_{xy}$ in the standard form. They take $2B = 7$ as $B = 7$ (instead of $B = 3.5$), leading to a wrong discriminant and wrong classification. **Always extract $B$ as the coefficient of $u_{xy}$ divided by 2.** > > 2. **Wrong Quadratic Formula** — Some students use $\xi = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{A}$ instead of $\xi = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - AC}}{A}$. The "$4AC$" version applies when the equation is $A\alpha^2 + B\alpha\beta + C\beta^2 = 0$ (no factor of 2). > > 3. **Mixing Up Time and Space** — A PDE like $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$ is **hyperbolic** in time, but the heat equation $u_t = \alpha u_{xx}$ is **parabolic**. The order of the time derivative is the key. > > 4. **Forgetting to State Units / Boundary Conditions** — When asked to "state the well-posed conditions," students often write only the IC and forget the two spatial BCs. > > 5. **Not Justifying the Classification** — KTU examiners want the discriminant **calculation shown**, not just the final answer. Always write: identify $A, B, C$ → compute $\Delta$ → conclude type. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — CLASSIFICATION OF PDEs** > > ✅ **Standard form**: $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} + D u_x + E u_y + F u = G$ > > ✅ **Discriminant**: $\Delta = B^2 - AC$ (with the **$2B$** convention) > > ✅ **Three Types**: > - **Elliptic** ($\Delta < 0$) → Steady-state → **Laplace / Poisson** → $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ > - **Parabolic** ($\Delta = 0$) → Diffusion → **Heat equation** → $u_t = \alpha u_{xx}$ > - **Hyperbolic** ($\Delta > 0$) → Waves → **Wave equation** → $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$ > > ✅ **Key Heat-Transfer Examples**: > - Steady heat conduction (no time) → **Elliptic** > - Transient heat conduction (first-order in time) → **Parabolic** > - Thermal shock / acoustic heating (second-order in time) → **Hyperbolic** > > ✅ **Boundary Conditions Required**: > - Elliptic: Closed boundary (Dirichlet/Neumann on all sides) > - Parabolic: 1 Initial Condition + 2 Spatial BCs > - Hyperbolic: 2 Initial Conditions + 2 Spatial BCs > > ✅ **The Discriminant is Invariant** under rotation/translation of axes — the classification is a **physical intrinsic property**, not a coordinate artifact. > > ✅ **Characteristic Directions** are found from $A \alpha^2 + 2B \alpha \beta + C \beta^2 = 0$: > - Elliptic: No real characteristics > - Parabolic: One repeated real characteristic > - Hyperbolic: Two distinct real characteristics > > ✅ **Forgetting the factor 2B** is the #1 reason students lose marks in this topic.

topics.title: Initial and Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET726 - COMPUTATIONAL HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Initial and Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Computational Heat Transfer**, the governing partial differential equations (PDEs) that describe conduction, convection, and radiation (such as the heat diffusion equation) are mathematically indeterminate in their pure differential form. To obtain a unique, physically meaningful solution, two supplementary classes of conditions must be appended to the PDE:

- **Initial Conditions (ICs):** The spatial distribution of the dependent variable (typically temperature, $T$) at the starting time instant ($t = 0$). ICs are mandatory only for **transient (unsteady)** problems.
- **Boundary Conditions (BCs):** The specified physical behavior of the dependent variable (or its spatial gradient) imposed on the geometric edges of the solution domain. BCs are mandatory for **both** transient and steady-state problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A problem is called *well-posed* (in the Hadamard sense) only if: **(1) a solution exists, (2) the solution is unique, and (3) the solution depends continuously on the input data.** ICs and BCs provide the uniqueness guarantee; without them, the PDE admits infinitely many solutions.

### 1.2 The Three Classical Types of Boundary Conditions

For a general second-order elliptic/parabolic PDE governing heat transfer, the three canonical BCs prescribed on the boundary surface $\Gamma$ are:

1. **Dirichlet Boundary Condition (Type 1 / First Kind):**
   Specifies the value of the dependent variable directly on the boundary.
   $$T(x, y, z, t) = f(x, y, z, t) \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma$$

2. **Neumann Boundary Condition (Type 2 / Second Kind):**
   Specifies the value of the normal derivative (heat flux) on the boundary.
   $$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = g(x, y, z, t) \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma$$

3. **Robin (Mixed) Boundary Condition (Type 3 / Third Kind / Cauchy):**
   Specifies a linear combination of the variable and its normal derivative — typically representing convective heat exchange at the surface.
   $$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} + h\,T = h\,T_\infty \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Locked Room" Puzzle:** Imagine a locked room where the air temperature tomorrow is unknown. The PDE is like the physics of air molecules bouncing around (a general rule). However, the *exact* temperature tomorrow depends on:
>
> 1. **What the room temperature was today** $\rightarrow$ this is the **Initial Condition**.
> 2. **What is happening at the walls** (is the door open to freezing air? is a heater blasting hot air onto one wall? is a wall perfectly insulated?) $\rightarrow$ these are the **Boundary Conditions**.
>
> Without knowing today's temperature (IC) and the wall conditions (BCs), you cannot uniquely determine tomorrow's temperature — the PDE alone gives infinitely many possible "rooms."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Boundary Case — Insulated/Adiabatic Surface:** A perfectly insulated boundary is a special Neumann BC where the normal heat flux is zero:
> $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = 0 \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma_{\text{adiabatic}}$$
> This is the mathematical statement of **Fourier's law** with zero conductive flux.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal diffusivity:** $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$, with units $\mathrm{m^2/s}$
- **Thermal conductivity:** $k$, with units $\mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$
- **Convective heat transfer coefficient:** $h$, with units $\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$
- **Biot number (dimensionless):** $\mathrm{Bi} = h L_c / k$ — quantifies the *ratio* of internal conduction resistance to surface convection resistance. $\mathrm{Bi} \to 0$ implies the lumped-capacitance assumption is valid, allowing omission of spatial ICs (only a uniform $T(t=0)$ is required).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic of a Well-Posed Heat Transfer Problem

For the transient 3-D heat conduction equation governing a solid domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\Gamma$:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{q} \quad \text{in} \quad \Omega$$

the **complete problem statement** requires the following logical hierarchy:

- **Step 1 — The PDE (Conservation Law):** Establishes the rate of energy storage equals net conduction in plus volumetric generation.
- **Step 2 — Geometric Domain ($\Omega$):** Defines the physical region of interest (a slab, cylinder, fin, turbine blade, etc.).
- **Step 3 — Initial Condition (IC):**
  Required only if $\partial T/\partial t \neq 0$. For a transient problem starting at $t=0$:
  $$T(x, y, z, 0) = T_0(x, y, z) \quad \text{in} \quad \Omega$$
- **Step 4 — Boundary Conditions (BCs):**
  Partition the boundary $\Gamma = \Gamma_1 \cup \Gamma_2 \cup \Gamma_3$, where $\Gamma_1, \Gamma_2, \Gamma_3$ are mutually disjoint subsets corresponding to the three BC types.
- **Step 5 — Material Property Specification:** Isotropic ($k$ = constant) vs. anisotropic ($k = [k_{ij}]$ tensor); constant vs. temperature-dependent.

### 2.2 Classification of Boundary Condition Categories

- **Why Dirichlet?** Used when the surface is held at a known temperature — e.g., a metal block immersed in a large ice bath, a boiler tube wall, or a phase-change interface at saturation temperature.
- **Why Neumann?** Used when the heat flux is known — e.g., electrical heating ($q'' = I^2 R / A$), nuclear fuel rods with known volumetric heating mapped to surface, or laser-irradiated surfaces.
- **Why Robin?** Used when the surface exchanges heat with a fluid at $T_\infty$ via convection — this is the **most common BC in engineering practice** because most real surfaces are convectively cooled/heated.

### 2.3 Initial Conditions — Detailed Treatment

For transient problems, the IC must be specified at *every* point in the domain $\Omega$:

$$T(x, y, z, t=0) = T_i(x, y, z)$$

Special cases in KTU-style problems:

- **Uniform IC:** $T_i(x,y,z) = T_0$ (constant)
- **Spatially varying IC:** Often a cosine or polynomial profile from a previous steady state
- **Lumped IC (when $\mathrm{Bi} \ll 0.1$):** The IC reduces to a single scalar value because temperature is assumed spatially uniform — this simplifies a 3-D PDE to an ODE.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Boundary & Initial Conditions

| Condition Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Engineering Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dirichlet (Type 1) | $T \vert_\Gamma = f$ | Surface temperature prescribed | Boiler wall, phase-change interface, isothermal fin base |
| Neumann (Type 2) | $-k\,\partial T/\partial n \vert_\Gamma = g$ | Heat flux prescribed | Electrical heater, laser surface treatment, solar absorber |
| Adiabatic (special Neumann) | $\partial T/\partial n \vert_\Gamma = 0$ | Zero flux (insulated) | Symmetry plane, far-field of fin tip, insulated pipe |
| Robin (Type 3) | $-k\,\partial T/\partial n + hT = hT_\infty$ | Convective exchange | Cooled turbine blade, fin in cross-flow, ambient-exposed fin |
| Interface (continuity) | $T_1 = T_2$ and $k_1\,\partial T_1/\partial n = k_2\,\partial T_2/\partial n$ | Perfect thermal contact | Composite wall, layered microelectronics |
| Initial Condition | $T(x,y,z,0) = T_i$ | Starting temperature field | Pre-heated workpiece, quenched component |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Note:** In the table above, $T \vert_\Gamma$ uses `\vert` (not the pipe symbol) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Reactor Design:** ICs represent the cold startup temperature distribution; BCs include the convective coolant (Robin) and the symmetric adiabatic planes (Neumann).
- **Electronics Thermal Management:** IC = ambient temperature at power-on; BCs include chip-to-heat-sink convection (Robin) and PCB edges (often Dirichlet or adiabatic).
- **Metal Casting & Welding:** IC = molten metal temperature field; BCs include mold-metal interface (Robin with $h$ depending on contact) and air-exposed surfaces (Robin with free-convection $h$).
- **Turbine Blade Cooling:** IC = uniform inlet temperature; BCs include internal convective cooling passages (Robin) and external hot-gas convection (Robin with $T_\infty = T_{\text{gas}}$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Generalized Robin Boundary Condition from Energy Balance

Consider a control volume of thickness $\Delta x$ adjacent to a solid surface exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$. Apply an energy balance on the surface:

**Step 1 — Heat conducted into the surface (Fourier's Law):**
$$q''_{\text{cond}} = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{surface}}$$

**Step 2 — Heat convected away from the surface (Newton's Law of Cooling):**
$$q''_{\text{conv}} = h (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 3 — Energy conservation at the surface (steady, no storage, no generation):**
$$q''_{\text{cond}} = q''_{\text{conv}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the two flux expressions into the balance:**
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{surface}} = h (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to the canonical Robin form:**
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} + h\,T_s = h\,T_\infty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **classic KTU 2-mark derivation question** and must be memorized for the Part A bank.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Heat Equation with Generalized Boundary Conditions

**Step 1 — Start from the integral energy balance on a differential element:**
$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} dV = \dot{q}\,dV + \oint_{\partial V} (k \nabla T) \cdot \hat{n}\,dA$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Divergence Theorem to convert the surface integral:**
$$\oint_{\partial V} (k \nabla T) \cdot \hat{n}\,dA = \int_V \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)\,dV$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single volume integral:**
$$\int_V \left[ \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} - \dot{q} - \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4 — Since the volume is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish (local form):**
$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{q}$$

**Step 5 — For constant $k$, this simplifies to the canonical heat equation:**
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{q}}{\rho c_p}$$

**Step 6 — Append the complete well-posed problem statement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} &= \alpha \nabla^2 T \quad \text{in} \quad \Omega,\ t > 0 \\[4pt]
T(x, y, z, 0) &= T_i(x, y, z) \quad \text{in} \quad \Omega \\[4pt]
T &= T_s \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma_1 \quad \text{(Dirichlet)} \\[4pt]
-k\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial n} &= q''_s \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma_2 \quad \text{(Neumann)} \\[4pt]
-k\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial n} + h T &= h T_\infty \quad \text{on} \quad \Gamma_3 \quad \text{(Robin)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Non-Dimensionalization Showing the Biot Number

**Step 1 — Define dimensionless variables:**
$$\theta = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}, \quad \tau = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2}, \quad X = \frac{x}{L_c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the 1-D transient heat equation with convective BC at $x = L_c$:**
$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \tau} = \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial X^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the convective BC at $X = 1$:**
$$-k \frac{1}{L_c}(T_i - T_\infty) \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial X}\bigg|_{X=1} = h (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange in terms of $\theta$ and isolate the dimensionless group:**
$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial X}\bigg|_{X=1} = \frac{h L_c}{k} \theta\big|_{X=1} = \mathrm{Bi} \cdot \theta\big|_{X=1}$$

**Step 5 — Result — the Biot number emerges naturally:**

$$\mathrm{Bi} = \frac{h L_c}{k}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:**
$$\mathrm{Bi} = \frac{\text{Internal conduction resistance}}{\text{Surface convection resistance}} = \frac{L_c / k}{1 / h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The Biot number derivation is a **frequently asked 7-mark problem** in Part B. The examiner looks for the *physical interpretation* of Bi — not just its definition.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Composite Slab with Mixed BCs

**Problem:** A 1-D plane wall of thickness $L$ has its left face ($x=0$) suddenly raised to $T_s$ and its right face ($x=L$) exposed to convection with $T_\infty$. Initially the wall is at $T_i$. Write the complete mathematical problem statement.

**Step 1 — Governing PDE (1-D, constant $k$, no source):**
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}, \quad 0 < x < L,\ t > 0$$

**Step 2 — Initial condition (uniform temperature):**
$$T(x, 0) = T_i, \quad 0 \le x \le L$$

**Step 3 — Boundary condition at $x=0$ (Dirichlet, prescribed temperature):**
$$T(0, t) = T_s, \quad t > 0$$

**Step 4 — Boundary condition at $x=L$ (Robin, convective):**
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = h \left[ T(L, t) - T_\infty \right], \quad t > 0$$

**Step 5 — Final compact problem statement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PDE:} \quad & \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}, \quad 0 < x < L,\ t > 0 \\[4pt]
\text{IC:} \quad & T(x, 0) = T_i \\[4pt]
\text{BC}_1: \quad & T(0, t) = T_s \\[4pt]
\text{BC}_2: \quad & k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} + h\,T(L, t) = h\,T_\infty
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Boundary Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boundary Conditions on Surface Gamma] --> B[Type 1 Dirichlet]
    A --> C[Type 2 Neumann]
    A --> D[Type 3 Robin Mixed]

    B --> B1[Prescribed Temperature T equals f]
    B1 --> B2[Examples Boiler wall Phase change interface]

    C --> C1[Prescribed Heat Flux minus k dT/dn equals g]
    C1 --> C2[Special Case Insulated dT/dn equals 0]
    C1 --> C3[Examples Electrical heater Laser irradiation]

    D --> D1[Convective Form minus k dT/dn plus hT equals hT infinity]
    D1 --> D2[Examples Fin in cross flow Cooled turbine blade Ambient exposed surface]

    E[Initial Conditions Required for Transient Only] --> E1[T of x y z at t equals 0 equals T initial]
    E1 --> E2[Uniform T initial equals constant]
    E1 --> E3[Spatially Varying Profile from prior steady state]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Well-Posed Problem Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Well Posed Heat Transfer Problem] --> P1[Step 1 Governing PDE]
    P --> P2[Step 2 Domain Definition]
    P --> P3[Step 3 Initial Condition for transient]
    P --> P4[Step 4 Boundary Conditions on all edges]
    P --> P5[Step 5 Material Property Closure]

    P1 --> P11[Energy Conservation rho cp dT/dt equals div of k grad T plus q dot]
    P2 --> P21[Region Omega and boundary Gamma]
    P3 --> P31[T at t equals 0 equals T initial of x y z]
    P4 --> P41[Dirichlet on Gamma 1]
    P4 --> P42[Neumann on Gamma 2]
    P4 --> P43[Robin on Gamma 3]
    P5 --> P51[Isotropic constant k or Anisotropic k tensor]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Convective BC Derivation (Energy Balance)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Conductive Flux into Surface] --> M[Energy Balance at Surface]
    S2[Convective Flux away from Surface] --> M
    M --> R[Robin BC minus k dT/dn plus hT equals hT infinity]
    L1[Special Case h equals 0] --> R2[Insulated BC dT/dn equals 0]
    R --> R2
    L2[Special Case h approaches infinity] --> R3[Dirichlet BC T equals T infinity]
    R --> R3
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Transient Problem Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Is the problem transient] -->|Yes| R1[Specify Initial Condition T at t equals 0]
    Q -->|No Steady State| R2[No IC required]
    R1 --> S1[Now apply BCs on all surfaces]
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Surface held at known T use Dirichlet]
    S1 --> T2[Surface with known heat flux use Neumann]
    S1 --> T3[Surface in convective fluid use Robin]
    S1 --> T4[Symmetry or insulated edge use adiabatic Neumann]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Classify the three classical types of boundary conditions used in computational heat transfer. For each type, give one physical example and write the governing equation on the boundary $\Gamma$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Equation on $\Gamma$ | Physical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Dirichlet (1st kind) | $T = f$ | Isothermal boiler wall, phase-change interface |
| Neumann (2nd kind) | $-k\,\partial T/\partial n = g$ | Electric resistance heater, laser-irradiated surface |
| Robin / Mixed (3rd kind) | $-k\,\partial T/\partial n + hT = hT_\infty$ | Convectively cooled fin, ambient-exposed turbine blade |

**Mark split:** 1 mark for naming each type with equation; 1 mark for the physical example; 1 mark for correct mathematical form.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*What is an initial condition? Why is it required only for transient problems and not for steady-state problems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **initial condition (IC)** is the prescribed spatial distribution of the dependent variable (typically temperature $T$) at the starting time $t = 0$, i.e., $T(x, y, z, 0) = T_i(x, y, z)$.

It is required only for **transient problems** because the governing equation $\rho c_p\,\partial T/\partial t = \nabla \cdot(k \nabla T) + \dot{q}$ contains a first-order time derivative. A first-order ODE in time requires exactly **one** initial condition per spatial point to integrate forward in time and obtain a unique solution.

In **steady-state** problems, $\partial T/\partial t = 0$, so the time derivative vanishes and the PDE reduces to an elliptic equation in space only. There is no temporal evolution to "start," and hence **no IC is needed** — only BCs.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for IC definition; 1 mark for first-order time derivative argument; 1 mark for steady-state reasoning.

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice A]**

**A(a) [7 marks]:** *Derive the Robin boundary condition from the first principles of energy balance at a solid surface exposed to a convective fluid.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the surface and draw a control volume** (1 mark): Consider a differential patch $dA$ of the solid surface at temperature $T_s$ exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$.

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier's law for conduction normal to the surface** (1 mark):
$$q''_{\text{cond}} = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg|_{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's law of cooling for convection away from the surface** (1 mark):
$$q''_{\text{conv}} = h\,(T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state energy balance at the surface** (1 mark): Energy in = Energy out, hence:
$$q''_{\text{cond}} = q''_{\text{conv}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and rearrange** (2 marks):
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg|_{s} = h\,(T_s - T_\infty) \implies -k\frac{\partial T}{\partial n} + hT_s = hT_\infty$$

**Step 6 — State the final canonical Robin BC and note the limiting cases** (1 mark): When $h \to 0$, the BC reduces to $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ (adiabatic). When $h \to \infty$, the BC reduces to $T = T_\infty$ (Dirichlet).

---

**A(b) [7 marks]:** *A long cylindrical nuclear fuel rod of radius $R$ initially at uniform temperature $T_i$ suddenly begins generating heat uniformly at rate $\dot{q}$ $\mathrm{W/m^3}$. The rod surface is cooled by a convective fluid at $T_\infty$ with coefficient $h$. Write the complete, well-posed mathematical problem statement in cylindrical coordinates.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify geometry and coordinates** (1 mark): Use cylindrical coordinates $(r, \phi, z)$ with axisymmetry ($\partial/\partial \phi = 0$) and axial symmetry (long rod, $\partial/\partial z = 0$). Hence $T = T(r, t)$.

**Step 2 — Governing PDE (transient, 1-D radial, with source)** (2 marks):
$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( k r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r} \right) + \dot{q}, \quad 0 < r < R,\ t > 0$$

**Step 3 — Initial condition** (1 mark):
$$T(r, 0) = T_i, \quad 0 \le r \le R$$

**Step 4 — Boundary condition at $r = 0$ (symmetry / regularity)** (1 mark):
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=0} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Boundary condition at $r = R$ (Robin / convective)** (1 mark):
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=R} = h\,\bigl[T(R, t) - T_\infty\bigr]$$

**Step 6 — Final compact statement** (1 mark):
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} &= \frac{k}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right) + \dot{q} \\[4pt]
T(r,0) &= T_i \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\big|_{r=0} &= 0 \\[4pt]
k\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\big|_{r=R} + h\,T(R,t) &= h\,T_\infty
\end{aligned}$$

---

### ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — QUESTION B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Choice B]

**B(a) [7 marks]:** *With the help of a suitable sketch and the divergence theorem, derive the 3-D transient heat conduction equation from the integral energy balance.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the integral energy balance on a control volume $V$ bounded by surface $A$** (1 mark):
$$\int_V \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\,dV = \int_V \dot{q}\,dV - \oint_A \vec{q}''\cdot \hat{n}\,dA$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Fourier's law $\vec{q}'' = -k\nabla T$** (1 mark):
$$\int_V \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\,dV = \int_V \dot{q}\,dV + \oint_A k\nabla T \cdot \hat{n}\,dA$$

**Step 3 — Apply the divergence theorem to convert surface to volume integral** (1 mark):
$$\oint_A k\nabla T \cdot \hat{n}\,dA = \int_V \nabla \cdot (k\nabla T)\,dV$$

**Step 4 — Combine integrals** (1 mark):
$$\int_V \left[ \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} - \dot{q} - \nabla \cdot (k\nabla T) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 5 — Since $V$ is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish locally** (1 mark):
$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k\nabla T) + \dot{q}$$

**Step 6 — For constant $k$, simplify and list the required IC/BCs** (2 marks):
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{q}}{\rho c_p}$$
With $T(\vec{r},0) = T_i$ (IC), and Dirichlet/Neumann/Robin BCs on $\Gamma$.

---

**B(b) [7 marks]:** *A semi-infinite solid initially at $T_i$ has its surface suddenly raised to $T_s$ at $t = 0$. Derive the well-posed problem and briefly describe the physical scenario.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physical scenario** (1 mark): Pre-heated metal slab plunged into a furnace — heat penetrates inward from the surface over time.

**Step 2 — Governing PDE (1-D, semi-infinite, no source)** (1 mark):
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}, \quad x > 0,\ t > 0$$

**Step 3 — Initial condition** (1 mark):
$$T(x, 0) = T_i, \quad x \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Boundary condition at $x = 0$ (Dirichlet)** (1 mark):
$$T(0, t) = T_s, \quad t > 0$$

**Step 5 — Boundary condition at $x \to \infty$ (far-field, undisturbed)** (1 mark):
$$T(x \to \infty, t) = T_i$$

**Step 6 — Discuss solution behavior and physical significance** (2 marks): Solution is a complementary error function. The thermal penetration depth grows as $\delta \sim \sqrt{\alpha t}$. The surface temperature jump $(T_s - T_i)$ propagates into the medium as a diffusing wave.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Neumann/Flux BC:** Many students write $+k\,\partial T/\partial n = g$ instead of $-k\,\partial T/\partial n = g$. The minus sign arises from Fourier's law where heat flows *down* the temperature gradient. **Lose 1–2 marks for this.**
> 2. **Confusing ICs with BCs:** A common mistake is specifying an IC on the boundary (e.g., $T(0,0) = T_i$) or a BC at $t=0$ (e.g., $T(x,0) = T_s$). ICs apply in the *interior* at $t=0$; BCs apply on the *spatial* boundary for $t > 0$. **Lose 1 mark for this conceptual confusion.**
> 3. **Forgetting to state the IC for a transient problem:** In Part B, transient 7-mark problems without an explicit IC are auto-docked **2 marks** by KTU examiners.
> 4. **Mixing up $h$ and $k$ in the Robin BC:** $h$ is the convective coefficient ($\mathrm{W/m^2 K}$), $k$ is the conductive coefficient ($\mathrm{W/m K}$). Conflating them forfeits the derivation marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Initial conditions** specify $T(x,y,z,0)$ — mandatory **only for transient** problems.
- **Boundary conditions** are mandatory for **all** PDEs (steady or transient).
- The **three BC types** are **Dirichlet** ($T$ specified), **Neumann** ($-k\,\partial T/\partial n$ specified), and **Robin** ($-k\,\partial T/\partial n + hT = hT_\infty$).
- A perfectly **insulated** surface corresponds to $\partial T/\partial n = 0$, which is a Neumann BC with $g = 0$.
- A **symmetry plane** also uses $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ because no heat can cross a plane of symmetry.
- A **well-posed** problem requires: (i) governing PDE, (ii) domain $\Omega$, (iii) IC (if transient), (iv) BCs on all of $\Gamma$, and (v) material property specification.
- The **Biot number** $\mathrm{Bi} = hL_c/k$ is the dimensionless group that emerges naturally from non-dimensionalizing the Robin BC.
- For **interface** continuity between two solids, both $T$ and $-k\,\partial T/\partial n$ must be continuous across the interface.
- Always check that the boundary $\Gamma$ is **fully partitioned** into $\Gamma_1 \cup \Gamma_2 \cup \Gamma_3$ with no gaps or overlaps.
- The **minus sign** in $-k\,\partial T/\partial n$ is non-negotiable — it comes from Fourier's law.
- In **lumped capacitance** ($\mathrm{Bi} \ll 0.1$), the IC reduces to a single scalar $T(t=0) = T_i$ and the spatial PDE collapses to an ODE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite Difference Form of PDE Equations – Taylor's Series Approach: Central, Backward and Forward Difference of First and Second Order Derivatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET726 - COMPUTATIONAL HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finite Difference Form of PDE Equations – Taylor's Series Approach: Central, Backward and Forward Difference of First and Second Order Derivatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** is a numerical technique used to approximate the solutions of Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by replacing continuous derivatives with discrete algebraic difference quotients evaluated on a structured grid of nodal points. The **Taylor's Series Approach** is the foundational mathematical framework used to derive these finite difference approximations. In this approach, the value of a function at a neighboring grid point is expressed as an infinite Taylor series expansion about a reference nodal point, and the series is truncated at a desired order to yield expressions for the derivatives.

In Computational Heat Transfer, every governing PDE (such as the 1-D heat conduction equation, the Laplace equation, or the Poisson equation) is first discretized using these finite difference approximations before being solved numerically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, students are expected to derive finite difference approximations for the **first-order derivative** $\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)$ and the **second-order derivative** $\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}\right)$ using the **Taylor's series expansion** method. The three fundamental schemes are:
> 1. **Forward Difference Scheme**
> 2. **Backward Difference Scheme**
> 3. **Central Difference Scheme**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speedometer" of a Function

Imagine you are driving a car along a curved road and want to know your **instantaneous speed** (the derivative) at the exact moment you are at point $x_i$. Since you cannot measure speed at a single instant with a regular speedometer, what do you do?

- **You look slightly ahead** and compare your position now with your position a moment later → This is the **Forward Difference** (approximating slope using the *next* point).
- **You look slightly behind** and compare your position now with your position a moment earlier → This is the **Backward Difference** (approximating slope using the *previous* point).
- **You average both** (look ahead and behind) → This is the **Central Difference**, which gives a much smoother, more accurate estimate, just like a sports car's traction control averages data from both sides.

Taylor's series is the mathematical "camera" that captures how the road curves, allowing us to compute these slopes using only the **discrete odometer readings** at grid points $x_{i-1}, x_i, x_{i+1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology in KTU Context:**
> - **Grid Point / Node ($x_i$):** A discrete location on the computational domain where the unknown variable $u$ is evaluated.
> - **Step Size ($\Delta x$ or $h$):** The uniform distance between adjacent grid points.
> - **Truncation Error ($\tau$):** The error introduced by neglecting the higher-order terms of the Taylor series.
> - **Order of Accuracy:** A scheme is *k-th order accurate* if the truncation error is proportional to $(\Delta x)^{k}$.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Difference Schemes

| Scheme Type | Uses Points | Order of Accuracy (1st Derivative) | Order of Accuracy (2nd Derivative) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Forward Difference (FDS)** | $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$ | $O(\Delta x)$ | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| **Backward Difference (BDS)** | $x_{i-1}$ and $x_i$ | $O(\Delta x)$ | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| **Central Difference (CDS)** | $x_{i-1}$ and $x_{i+1}$ | $O(\Delta x)^{2}$ | $O(\Delta x)^{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taylor Series Theorem — Foundation of FDM

For a smooth, continuous, and infinitely differentiable function $u(x)$, the value of the function at a point $x = x_i + \Delta x$ (or $x_i - \Delta x$) can be expanded about the reference point $x_i$ as a polynomial series.

**General Taylor Series Expansion about $x_i$:**

$$u(x_i + \Delta x) = u(x_i) + \Delta x \cdot \frac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{2}}{2!} \cdot \frac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{3}}{3!} \cdot \frac{d^{3}u}{dx^{3}}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{4}}{4!} \cdot \frac{d^{4}u}{dx^{4}}\bigg|_{x_i} + \cdots$$

By convention, let us denote the step size as $\Delta x = h$ and use a shorthand notation:

- $u_i = u(x_i)$
- $u_{i+1} = u(x_i + h)$
- $u_{i-1} = u(x_i - h)$
- $u'_i = \dfrac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{x_i}$, $u''_i = \dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}\bigg|_{x_i}$, and so on.

**Expanded Taylor Series (Forward Direction):**

$$u_{i+1} = u_i + h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Expanded Taylor Series (Backward Direction):**

$$u_{i-1} = u_i - h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i - \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Each Scheme

### Step 1: Forward Difference Approximation (1st Derivative)
- **Why?** Start from the forward Taylor series.
- **How?** Solve algebraically for $u'_i$ and **truncate all higher-order terms** to obtain a usable approximation.

### Step 2: Backward Difference Approximation (1st Derivative)
- **Why?** Use the backward Taylor series.
- **How?** Rearrange to isolate $u'_i$ and truncate.

### Step 3: Central Difference Approximation (1st Derivative)
- **Why?** Subtracting the backward series from the forward series *cancels all even-order derivatives* automatically.
- **How?** Subtract and solve for $u'_i$.

### Step 4: Second Derivative Approximation
- **Why?** Adding the two Taylor series *cancels all odd-order derivatives*.
- **How?** Add the forward and backward expansions and solve for $u''_i$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU board questions from this module can be solved using the master table below.** Memorize it!

| Derivative | Scheme | Finite Difference Formula | Truncation Error $\tau$ | Accuracy |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\dfrac{du}{dx}$ | **Forward** | $\dfrac{du}{dx} \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{h}$ | $-\dfrac{h}{2} u''_i$ | $O(h)$ |
| $\dfrac{du}{dx}$ | **Backward** | $\dfrac{du}{dx} \approx \dfrac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{h}$ | $+\dfrac{h}{2} u''_i$ | $O(h)$ |
| $\dfrac{du}{dx}$ | **Central** | $\dfrac{du}{dx} \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2h}$ | $-\dfrac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i$ | $O(h^{2})$ |
| $\dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}$ | **Central** | $\dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}} \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^{2}}$ | $-\dfrac{h^{2}}{12} u^{(4)}_i$ | $O(h^{2})$ |
| $\dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}$ | **Forward** | $\dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}} \approx \dfrac{u_{i+2} - 2u_{i+1} + u_i}{h^{2}}$ | $+h \cdot u'''_i$ | $O(h)$ |
| $\dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}$ | **Backward** | $\dfrac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}} \approx \dfrac{u_i - 2u_{i-1} + u_{i-2}}{h^{2}}$ | $-h \cdot u'''_i$ | $O(h)$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Computational Heat Transfer, these difference approximations are the **building blocks** of every numerical solver:
- The **central difference** for $\dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$ is the heart of the explicit and implicit schemes used to solve the 1-D heat equation.
- The **forward difference in time** combined with **central difference in space** yields the famous **FTCS (Forward Time Central Space)** scheme.
- In CFD codes like ANSYS Fluent or OpenFOAM, these very same Taylor-derived stencils are used in the interior of the computational domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used:** Let $h = \Delta x$ be the uniform grid spacing. The notation $u^{(n)}_i$ denotes the $n$-th derivative of $u$ evaluated at node $i$.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Difference for First-Order Derivative

**Starting Equation (Forward Taylor Series):**

$$u_{i+1} = u_i + h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2!} u''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{3!} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{4!} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 1:** Subtract $u_i$ from both sides.

$$u_{i+1} - u_i = h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Divide the entire equation by $h$.

$$\frac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{h} = u'_i + \frac{h}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Isolate $u'_i$ on the left-hand side.

$$u'_i = \frac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{h} - \frac{h}{2} u''_i - \frac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i - \frac{h^{3}}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Truncate after the leading term. The remainder is the **truncation error** $\tau_f$.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{i, \text{forward}} = \frac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{h} + O(h)}$$

where $\tau_f = -\dfrac{h}{2} u''_i$ is the leading truncation error, confirming first-order accuracy $O(h)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backward Difference for First-Order Derivative

**Starting Equation (Backward Taylor Series):**

$$u_{i-1} = u_i - h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2!} u''_i - \frac{h^{3}}{3!} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{4!} u^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 1:** Subtract $u_i$ from both sides.

$$u_{i-1} - u_i = -h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i - \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Divide the entire equation by $h$.

$$\frac{u_{i-1} - u_i}{h} = -u'_i + \frac{h}{2} u''_i - \frac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $-1$ to obtain the positive derivative.

$$\frac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{h} = u'_i - \frac{h}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i - \frac{h^{3}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $u'_i$ on the LHS and truncate.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{i, \text{backward}} = \frac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{h} + O(h)}$$

with leading truncation error $\tau_b = +\dfrac{h}{2} u''_i$, also first-order accurate $O(h)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Central Difference for First-Order Derivative

**Step 1:** Write down both the forward and backward Taylor series.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} &= u_i + h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{h^{5}}{120} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots \\
u_{i-1} &= u_i - h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i - \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \frac{h^{5}}{120} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the second equation from the first.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} - u_{i-1} &= (u_i - u_i) + (h u'_i - (-h u'_i)) + \left(\frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i - \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i\right) + \left(\frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i - \left(-\frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i\right)\right) \\
&\quad + \left(\frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i\right) + \left(\frac{h^{5}}{120} u^{(5)}_i - \left(-\frac{h^{5}}{120} u^{(5)}_i\right)\right) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify. Notice that all **even-order** derivatives cancel out, leaving only odd-order terms.

$$u_{i+1} - u_{i-1} = 2h \cdot u'_i + \frac{2h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{2h^{5}}{120} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

$$u_{i+1} - u_{i-1} = 2h \cdot u'_i + \frac{h^{3}}{3} u'''_i + \frac{h^{5}}{60} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $2h$.

$$\frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2h} = u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{120} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 5:** Isolate $u'_i$ and truncate.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{i, \text{central}} = \frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2h} + O(h^{2})}$$

Leading truncation error $\tau_c = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{6} u'''_i$, giving **second-order accuracy** $O(h^{2})$ — twice as accurate as forward/backward schemes.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Central Difference for Second-Order Derivative

**Step 1:** Use the same two Taylor expansions. This time, **add** them instead of subtracting.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} &= 2 u_i + 2 \cdot \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i + 2 \cdot \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + 2 \cdot \frac{h^{6}}{720} u^{(6)}_i + \cdots \\
u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} &= 2 u_i + h^{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{12} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{h^{6}}{360} u^{(6)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract $2 u_i$ from both sides.

$$u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} - 2 u_i = h^{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{12} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{h^{6}}{360} u^{(6)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $h^{2}$.

$$\frac{u_{i+1} - 2 u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^{2}} = u''_i + \frac{h^{2}}{12} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{h^{4}}{360} u^{(6)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $u''_i$ and truncate.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}\right)_{i, \text{central}} = \frac{u_{i+1} - 2 u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^{2}} + O(h^{2})}$$

This is the most widely used second-derivative approximation in heat transfer (appears in 1-D conduction, Laplace, and Poisson equations). The leading truncation error is $\tau = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{12} u^{(4)}_i$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Forward Difference for Second-Order Derivative

**Step 1:** Use the forward Taylor series twice — once at $x_{i+1}$ and once at $x_{i+2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} &= u_i + h u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{h^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots \\
u_{i+2} &= u_i + 2h u'_i + \frac{(2h)^{2}}{2} u''_i + \frac{(2h)^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \frac{(2h)^{4}}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the second expansion.

$$u_{i+2} = u_i + 2h u'_i + 2h^{2} u''_i + \frac{4h^{3}}{3} u'''_i + \frac{2h^{4}}{3} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Form the combination $u_{i+2} - 2 u_{i+1}$ to eliminate the $u'_i$ term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+2} - 2 u_{i+1} &= \left(u_i + 2h u'_i + 2h^{2} u''_i + \cdots\right) - 2\left(u_i + h u'_i + \frac{h^{2}}{2} u''_i + \frac{h^{3}}{6} u'''_i + \cdots\right) \\
u_{i+2} - 2 u_{i+1} &= (u_i - 2u_i) + (2h - 2h) u'_i + \left(2h^{2} - h^{2}\right) u''_i + \left(\frac{4h^{3}}{3} - \frac{h^{3}}{3}\right) u'''_i + \cdots \\
u_{i+2} - 2 u_{i+1} &= -u_i + h^{2} u''_i + h^{3} u'''_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Add $u_i$ to both sides.

$$u_{i+2} - 2 u_{i+1} + u_i = h^{2} u''_i + h^{3} u'''_i + \cdots$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $h^{2}$ and isolate $u''_i$.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}\right)_{i, \text{forward}} = \frac{u_{i+2} - 2u_{i+1} + u_i}{h^{2}} + O(h)}$$

Notice that the truncation error is $O(h)$ — only **first-order accurate**, because the $u'''_i$ term did not cancel.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Backward Difference for Second-Order Derivative

By symmetry, replacing the index $i \to i$ and using the points $u_{i-1}$ and $u_{i-2}$:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}}\right)_{i, \text{backward}} = \frac{u_i - 2u_{i-1} + u_{i-2}}{h^{2}} + O(h)}$$

This is also first-order accurate $O(h)$, with truncation error $\tau = -h \cdot u'''_i$.

## 3.7 Higher-Order Compact Form (Richardson Extrapolation Preview)

If both $O(h)$ and $O(h^{2})$ approximations are available, a **Richardson extrapolation** can eliminate the next error term to yield $O(h^{4})$ accuracy:

$$\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{\text{improved}} = \frac{4 \cdot \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{CDS} - \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{FDS}}{3} + O(h^{2})$$

This is a popular KTU viva question and frequently appears as a 3-mark Part-A item.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs use alphanumeric prefixed names (e.g., `step1`, `nodeA`) and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric characters to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Derivation Workflow of Finite Difference Approximations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START: TAYLOR SERIES ABOUT NODE i] --> B[WRITE FORWARD EXPANSION u_i_plus_1]
    A --> C[WRITE BACKWARD EXPANSION u_i_minus_1]
    A --> D[WRITE EXPANSION AT u_i_plus_2 OR u_i_minus_2]
    B --> E[FIRST ORDER FDS: SUBTRACT u_i AND DIVIDE BY h]
    C --> F[FIRST ORDER BDS: SUBTRACT u_i AND DIVIDE BY h]
    B --> G[CDS FIRST ORDER: SUBTRACT EXPANSIONS CANCELS EVEN TERMS]
    C --> G
    B --> H[CDS SECOND ORDER: ADD EXPANSIONS CANCELS ODD TERMS]
    C --> H
    D --> I[FDS SECOND ORDER: COMBINE u_i_plus_2 AND u_i_plus_1]
    D --> J[BDS SECOND ORDER: COMBINE u_i_minus_2 AND u_i_minus_1]
    E --> K[OUTPUT: FDS O h ACCURATE]
    F --> L[OUTPUT: BDS O h ACCURATE]
    G --> M[OUTPUT: CDS O h SQUARED ACCURATE]
    H --> N[OUTPUT: CDS SECOND DERIVATIVE O h SQUARED]
    I --> O[OUTPUT: FDS SECOND DERIVATIVE O h]
    J --> P[OUTPUT: BDS SECOND DERIVATIVE O h]
    M --> Q[APPLY TO HEAT EQUATION LAPLACE POISSON]
    N --> Q
    Q --> R[FINAL: NUMERICAL SOLUTION ON GRID]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[NODE i MINUS 2] --> B[NODE i MINUS 1]
    B --> C[NODE i CENTER]
    C --> D[NODE i PLUS 1]
    D --> E[NODE i PLUS 2]
    B -.->|DISTANCE h| C
    C -.->|DISTANCE h| D
    A -.->|DISTANCE 2h| C
    C -.->|DISTANCE 2h| E
    A -.->|DISTANCE 3h| E
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[PHYSICAL DOMAIN: ROD OF LENGTH L] --> P2[DISCRETIZE INTO N NODES]
    P2 --> P3[APPLY CENTRAL DIFFERENCE FOR d2T by dx2]
    P3 --> P4[APPLY FDS OR BDS FOR dT by dt]
    P4 --> P5[ASSEMBLE TRIDIAGONAL SYSTEM OF EQUATIONS]

```
