## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Discretised Momentum Equation (x-Direction)

Starting from the steady Navier–Stokes equation and integrating over the control volume surrounding node $P$ on a **staggered grid** (so that $u$ is stored at the east face), we obtain the standard five-point discretised form:

$$
a_{P}\,u_{P} = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\,u_{nb} + (p_{P} - p_{E})\,A_{e} + b_{P}
$$

- $a_{P}$: central coefficient
- $a_{nb}$: neighbour coefficients (E, W, N, S)
- $A_{e}$: area of east face
- $b_{P}$: source / body-force term

### 3.2 Velocity Decomposition (Theoretical Core)

Write the **intermediate velocity** $u_{P}$ as the sum of (i) a velocity computed **without** the pressure-gradient contribution, called $\hat{u}_{P}$, plus (ii) the contribution due to the local pressure difference:

$$
\hat{u}_{P} \equiv \frac{1}{a_{P}}\!\left(\sum a_{nb}u_{nb} + b_{P}\right)
$$

$$
u_{P} = \hat{u}_{P} + \frac{A_{e}}{a_{P}}\,(p_{P} - p_{E})
$$

Define the **velocity-coupling coefficient** at the east face:

$$
d_{e} \equiv \frac{A_{e}}{a_{P,\,e}}
$$

Then the east-face velocity $u_{e}$ — the one that actually appears in the continuity balance — is:

$$
u_{e} = \hat{u}_{e} + d_{e}\,(p_{P} - p_{E})
$$

Analogous expressions hold for $v_{n}$, $v_{s}$, $w_{t}$, $w_{b}$.

### 3.3 Substituting into Continuity (Full Derivation of Pressure-Correction Equation)

The integral continuity equation over the control volume of node $P$ reads:

$$
(\rho u A)_{e} - (\rho u A)_{w} + (\rho v A)_{n} - (\rho v A)_{s} = 0
$$

Now **substitute the velocity decomposition** for each face. With constant $\rho$ and area $A$:

$$
\rho A_{e}\bigl[\hat{u}_{e} + d_{e}(p_{P} - p_{E})\bigr]
- \rho A_{w}\bigl[\hat{u}_{w} + d_{w}(p_{P} - p_{W})\bigr]
$$
$$
+\;\rho A_{n}\bigl[\hat{v}_{n} + d_{n}(p_{P} - p_{N})\bigr]
- \rho A_{s}\bigl[\hat{v}_{s} + d_{s}(p_{P} - p_{S})\bigr]
= 0
$$

Grouping the **pressure terms** on the left and moving the **hatted (known) velocities** to the right:

$$
\rho\Bigl[d_{e}(p_{P}-p_{E}) + d_{w}(p_{P}-p_{W}) + d_{n}(p_{P}-p_{N}) + d_{s}(p_{P}-p_{S})\Bigr]
$$
$$
= -\Bigl[(\rho \hat{u}A)_{e} - (\rho \hat{u}A)_{w} + (\rho \hat{v}A)_{n} - (\rho \hat{v}A)_{s}\Bigr]
$$

Re-arranging into the canonical five-point Laplacian form:

$$
\boxed{a_{P}\,p_{P} = a_{E}\,p_{E} + a_{W}\,p_{W} + a_{N}\,p_{N} + a_{S}\,p_{S} + b}
$$

with the **coefficients**:

$$
a_{E} = \rho\,d_{e}\,A_{e}, \qquad a_{W} = \rho\,d_{w}\,A_{w}, \qquad a_{N} = \rho\,d_{n}\,A_{n}, \qquad a_{S} = \rho\,d_{s}\,A_{s}
$$

$$
a_{P} = a_{E} + a_{W} + a_{N} + a_{S}
$$

and the **mass-imbalance source**:

$$
b = (\rho \hat{u}A)_{w} - (\rho \hat{u}A)_{e} + (\rho \hat{v}A)_{s} - (\rho \hat{v}A)_{n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For incompressible flow at constant $\rho$, the unknowns in the discretised system are **only the pressure corrections**, and the matrix is symmetric positive-definite (SPD), enabling efficient solvers (TDMA, conjugate gradient, multigrid).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Why the Pressure-Correction Equation Works

| Step | Physical Action | Mathematical Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Solve momentum with $p^{*}$ | Predicts velocity ignoring continuity | $u^{*}, v^{*}, w^{*}$ generally not divergence-free |
| Compute $b$ (mass imbalance) | Quantifies continuity violation | $b \neq 0$ ⇒ residual |
| Solve $\nabla^{2}p' = b$ | Diffuses correction across whole domain | Each cell receives pressure correction proportional to its imbalance |
| Update $p, u, v, w$ | Enforces both momentum and continuity | New iterate is closer to the true solution |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the SIMPLE Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialise Field p star, u star, v star, w star] --> B[Set Iteration Counter iter = 1]
    B --> C[Solve x Momentum Equation for u star]
    C --> D[Solve y Momentum Equation for v star]
    D --> E[Solve z Momentum Equation for w star]
    E --> F[Compute Mass Imbalance Source b at each CV]
    F --> G[Assemble Pressure Correction Matrix aP p prime = sum a nb p nb + b]
    G --> H[Solve Linear System for p prime]
    H --> I[Correct Pressure: p new = p star + alpha p times p prime]
    I --> J[Correct Velocities: u new = u star + d e times p prime P minus p prime E]
    J --> K[Apply Under Relaxation to u v w with alpha u]
    K --> L{Check Convergence: Residuals less than epsilon}
    L -- No --> M[iter = iter + 1, Set p star = p new, u star = u new]
    M --> C
    L -- Yes --> N[Stop: Solution Converged]
```

### 4.2 Pressure-Velocity Coupling Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSTAR[Guessed Pressure p star] --> MOM[Solve Momentum Equations]
    MOM --> USTAR[Provisional Velocity u star, v star, w star]
    USTAR --> CONT[Plug into Continuity]
    CONT --> MASSIMB[Compute Mass Imbalance b]
    MASSIMB --> POIS[Solve Pressure Correction Equation]
    POIS --> PP[Pressure Correction p prime]
    PP --> UP[Update p and u, v, w]
    UP --> CHECK{Converged}
    CHECK -- No --> PSTAR
    CHECK -- Yes --> DONE[Final Solution]
```

### 4.3 Staggered Grid Layout (Node Identification)

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[Node N] --- n2[Node NE]
    n2 --- n3[Node E]
    n3 --- n4[Node SE]
    n4 --- n5[Node S]
    n5 --- n6[Node SW]
    n6 --- n7[Node W]
    n7 --- n8[Node NW]
    n8 --- n1
    n2 --- uE[u velocity stored at east face]
    n7 --- uW[u velocity stored at west face]
    n2 --- vN[v velocity stored at north face]
    n5 --- vS[v velocity stored at south face]
    n3 --- pE[Pressure node P east]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On a **staggered grid**, $u$-velocities are stored on vertical cell faces, $v$-velocities on horizontal faces, and pressures at cell centres. This naturally avoids the **checkerboard pressure oscillations** that plague collocated grids.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define the SIMPLE algorithm. State its full form and name the scientists who introduced it. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
> **SIMPLE** stands for **Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations**. It is an iterative procedure for solving the coupled pressure–velocity field of the **incompressible Navier–Stokes equations**. It was proposed by **S. V. Patankar and D. B. Spalding** in **1972** in their seminal paper *"A calculation procedure for heat, mass and momentum transfer in three-dimensional parabolic flows"*. The algorithm introduces a *pressure correction* variable $p'$, derived from the continuity residual, to enforce mass conservation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series I]** Why is a separate pressure equation not directly available in incompressible flow, and how does SIMPLE overcome this difficulty? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
In incompressible flow, the continuity equation reduces to $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, which is a **kinematic constraint** and not a transport equation for pressure. Hence pressure appears only as a source term in the momentum equations, creating a **pressure–velocity coupling problem**.
SIMPLE overcomes this by:
1. **Guessing** a pressure field $p^{*}$ and solving momentum to obtain intermediate velocities $u^{*}, v^{*}, w^{*}$.
2. **Forming** a pressure-correction equation of Poisson type from the continuity residual.
3. **Correcting** $p$ and $\vec{V}$ iteratively until both momentum and continuity are satisfied simultaneously.

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** Derive the pressure-correction equation used in the SIMPLE algorithm starting from the discretised momentum and continuity equations on a staggered grid.

#### (a) Discretised momentum and velocity decomposition — **7 marks**

**Step 1.** Write the steady Navier–Stokes x-momentum equation integrated over a control volume. On a staggered grid, the discretised form is:

$$
a_{P}u_{P} = a_{E}u_{E} + a_{W}u_{W} + a_{N}u_{N} + a_{S}u_{S} + (p_{P} - p_{E})A_{e} + b_{P}
$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange to separate the velocity-without-pressure-gradient $\hat{u}_{P}$:

$$
\hat{u}_{P} \equiv \frac{a_{E}u_{E} + a_{W}u_{W} + a_{N}u_{N} + a_{S}u_{S} + b_{P}}{a_{P}}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore the node-P velocity becomes:

$$
u_{P} = \hat{u}_{P} + \frac{A_{e}}{a_{P}}(p_{P} - p_{E})
$$

**Step 4.** Define the east-face velocity-coupling coefficient:

$$
d_{e} = \frac{A_{e}}{a_{P,\,e}}
$$

so that the east-face velocity is:

$$
u_{e} = \hat{u}_{e} + d_{e}(p_{P} - p_{E})
$$

Similar expressions hold for $v_{n}, v_{s}$ on horizontal faces.

*(This completes part (a) for 7 marks.)*

#### (b) Continuity substitution and pressure-correction equation — **7 marks**

**Step 5.** The integral continuity over the control volume of node P reads:

$$
(\rho u A)_{e} - (\rho u A)_{w} + (\rho v A)_{n} - (\rho v A)_{s} = 0
$$

**Step 6.** Substituting the velocity decomposition:

$$
\rho A_{e}\bigl[\hat{u}_{e} + d_{e}(p_{P} - p_{E})\bigr]
- \rho A_{w}\bigl[\hat{u}_{w} + d_{w}(p_{P} - p_{W})\bigr]
$$
$$
+\;\rho A_{n}\bigl[\hat{v}_{n} + d_{n}(p_{P} - p_{N})\bigr]
- \rho A_{s}\bigl[\hat{v}_{s} + d_{s}(p_{P} - p_{S})\bigr] = 0
$$

**Step 7.** Group pressure terms and move hatted velocities to the right:

$$
\rho\Bigl[d_{e}(p_{P}-p_{E}) + d_{w}(p_{P}-p_{W}) + d_{n}(p_{P}-p_{N}) + d_{s}(p_{P}-p_{S})\Bigr] = b
$$

**Step 8.** Identify coefficients:

$$
a_{E} = \rho d_{e}A_{e},\quad a_{W} = \rho d_{w}A_{w},\quad a_{N} = \rho d_{n}A_{n},\quad a_{S} = \rho d_{s}A_{s}
$$

$$
a_{P} = a_{E} + a_{W} + a_{N} + a_{S}
$$

$$
b = (\rho \hat{u}A)_{w} - (\rho \hat{u}A)_{e} + (\rho \hat{v}A)_{s} - (\rho \hat{v}A)_{n}
$$

**Step 9.** The pressure-correction equation becomes:

$$
\boxed{a_{P}\,p_{P} = a_{E}\,p_{E} + a_{W}\,p_{W} + a_{N}\,p_{N} + a_{S}\,p_{S} + b}
$$

*(This completes part (b) for 7 marks, taking total to 14.)*

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks — *Internal Choice*)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** Explain in detail the step-by-step iterative procedure of the SIMPLE algorithm. Discuss the role of under-relaxation factors and mention two popular variants.

#### (a) Algorithm procedure — **7 marks**

The SIMPLE algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Initialisation:** Guess the pressure field $p^{*}$, the velocity field $(u^{*}, v^{*}, w^{*})$ and all other scalar variables.
2. **Momentum solution:** Solve the discretised momentum equations using $p^{*}$ to obtain provisional velocities $u^{*}, v^{*}, w^{*}$.
3. **Mass-imbalance computation:** Evaluate the continuity residual at each control volume, $b_{P} = (\rho u^{*}A)_{w} - (\rho u^{*}A)_{e} + (\rho v^{*}A)_{s} - (\rho v^{*}A)_{n}$.
4. **Pressure-correction solve:** Assemble and solve the Poisson-type equation $a_{P}p'_{P} = \sum a_{nb}p'_{nb} + b$.
5. **Corrections:** Update pressure and velocities:
   - $p \leftarrow p^{*} + \alpha_{p}p'$
   - $u \leftarrow u^{*} + d_{e}(p'_{P} - p'_{E})$
6. **Convergence check:** Compute normalised residuals of momentum, continuity, and (if applicable) energy. If all are below tolerance (typically $10^{-6}$), **stop**; else set $p^{*}\leftarrow p$, $u^{*}\leftarrow u$, and go to step 2.

*(This completes part (a) for 7 marks.)*

#### (b) Under-relaxation and variants — **7 marks**

**Under-relaxation factors:**
- The pressure-correction equation over-corrects because it ignores neighbouring corrections. To stabilise, a **pressure under-relaxation factor** $\alpha_{p}$ (typically **0.3**) is applied:
  $p^{new} = p^{*} + \alpha_{p}\,p'$
- Similarly, a **velocity under-relaxation** $\alpha_{u}$ (typically **0.5 – 0.7**) is applied to dampen the velocity update.
- These factors **trade convergence speed for stability** — small $\alpha$ improves robustness but slows convergence.

**Two popular variants:**
1. **SIMPLEC (SIMPLE-Consistent):** Proposed by Van Doormaal and Raithby (1984). It modifies the velocity-coupling coefficient to account for the omitted neighbour-correction term, allowing larger $\alpha_{p}$ (≈ 1.0) and **faster convergence**.
2. **PISO (Pressure-Implicit with Splitting of Operators):** Proposed by Issa (1986). It uses **one predictor and two corrector steps** within each iteration, eliminating the need for outer iteration on the pressure-correction equation — particularly effective for **transient** flows.

*(This completes part (b) for 7 marks, taking total to 14.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Forgetting to substitute the velocity decomposition into continuity** before simplifying — examiners deduct up to **4 marks** if Step 6 of the derivation is skipped.
> - **Confusing starred ($^{*}$) and primed ($'$) variables.** Starred = current iterate; Primed = correction to be added. Mixing these up invalidates the entire derivation.
> - **Omitting the under-relaxation step** in the algorithm. Even though it appears numerical, examiners expect $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{u}$ explicitly mentioned for full marks.
> - **Writing the pressure-correction equation without the source term $b$.** The mass-imbalance $b$ is the very heart of SIMPLE and must appear explicitly.
> - **Not stating Patankar and Spalding (1972)** as the originators of SIMPLE — a guaranteed loss of 1 mark in definition-type questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SIMPLE = Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations**, developed by **Patankar & Spalding (1972)**.
- It is an **iterative** algorithm for the **pressure–velocity coupling** in **incompressible Navier–Stokes** equations.
- **Starred ($^{*}$) values** = current iterate; **primed ($'$) values** = correction to be added.
- The **pressure-correction equation** is a Poisson equation of the form $a_{P}p'_{P} = \sum a_{nb}p'_{nb} + b$, where $b$ is the **continuity residual**.
- **Under-relaxation** is essential: $\alpha_{p} \approx 0.3$, $\alpha_{u} \approx 0.5\text{–}0.7$.
- **Staggered grids** avoid checkerboard pressure oscillations; on **collocated grids**, the **Rhie–Chow interpolation** is required.
- **Variants to remember:** **SIMPLEC** (Van Doormaal & Raithby, 1984) — faster steady-state convergence; **PISO** (Issa, 1986) — better for transient flows.
- **Convergence criterion:** normalised residual of mass and momentum < **10⁻⁶**.
- **Real-world codes using SIMPLE:** ANSYS Fluent (default), OpenFOAM `simpleFoam`, STAR-CD, CFX.
